old_claim.note: Lithium-Ion Batteries (Rechargeable Power) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Implantable pacemakers need a self-contained battery power source; lithium-ion cells are later battery technology, not the original prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: ACC history of battery-powered portable pacemakers and the first implanted pacemaker in 1958.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes battery-powered portable cardiac pacing in 1957 and the first implanted pacemaker in 1958, before modern lithium-ion cells.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a portable-battery edge while rejecting lithium-ion as the historical prerequisite because the early devices used self-contained battery power before lithium-ion technology existed.

ontology_before: Pacemakers depended on lithium-ion batteries, creating a false later-technology dependency for a 1958 implantable device.
ontology_after: Pacemakers depend on portable electrochemical battery power; lithium-ion remains a later battery family and not the original prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the lithium-ion edge is absent and replaced with general portable electrochemical batteries.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to modern rechargeable lithium-ion pacemaker systems only.
A source showed the first implantable pacemakers required lithium-ion cells.
The graph removed self-contained battery power from implantable pacemakers.
short_reason: Pacemakers need battery power, but early implantable pacemakers did not depend on lithium-ion batteries.
